tasks.title: Warm-up: Creating and Working with Namespaces
tasks.goal: Create Namespaces using two methods (imperative / declarative YAML), then practice targeting a specific namespace with the -n flag and set-context.
tasks.prompt:
1. Create the team-a namespace imperatively.
2. Write a YAML manifest (ns-team-b.yaml) and create the team-b namespace declaratively.
3. Run kubectl get ns to confirm both namespaces are listed.
4. Create a Pod named web-pod using the nginx image inside team-a. (Use kubectl run with the -n flag.)
5. Run kubectl get pods -n team-a to confirm the Pod is in the Running state.
6. Change the default namespace to team-a with kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=team-a, then run kubectl get pods without -n and confirm you see the same result.
7. When finished, restore the default namespace to default.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create team-a imperatively
kubectl create namespace ____

# 2. Write ns-team-b.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ____
metadata:
  name: ____

# 3. Apply declaratively
kubectl apply -f ns-team-b.yaml

# 4. Create a Pod inside team-a
kubectl run web-pod --image=nginx ____ team-a

# 5. Verify
kubectl get pods ____ team-a

# 6. Change the default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current ____=team-a
kubectl get pods   # without -n

# 7. Restore
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=default
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create team-a imperatively
kubectl create namespace team-a

# 2. ns-team-b.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: team-b

# Apply declaratively
kubectl apply -f ns-team-b.yaml

# 3. Verify all namespaces
kubectl get ns
# team-a and team-b should appear in Active state

# 4. Create a Pod inside team-a
kubectl run web-pod --image=nginx -n team-a

# 5. Check Pod status
kubectl get pods -n team-a
# NAME      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# web-pod   1/1     Running   0          ...

# 6. Change default namespace and confirm same result without -n
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=team-a
kubectl get pods
# web-pod is shown the same as before

# 7. Restore default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=default
tasks.explanation: Namespace is a top-level v1 API object and can be created with kubectl create namespace or a YAML manifest. Objects with the same name can coexist without conflict as long as they are in different namespaces. The -n flag targets a specific namespace for a single command, while set-context --namespace changes the default namespace for the current context so it applies to all subsequent commands automatically. Always restore to default when done to avoid confusion in later steps.
hints: kubectl create namespace and kind: Namespace produce the same result. The kind value is case-sensitive — it must be 'Namespace', not 'namespace'.
kubectl run accepts -n <name> or --namespace=<name>. You can also write --namespace team-a with a space instead of =.
After running set-context, use kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace to verify the default namespace actually changed.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Applying LimitRange — Confirming Automatic Default Injection
tasks.goal: Apply a LimitRange to the team-a namespace, confirm that default values are automatically injected into a Pod that specifies no resources block at all, and observe that a container exceeding max is rejected.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write limit-range.yaml with the following spec and apply it to team-a.
   - type: Container
   - defaultRequest: cpu=100m, memory=128Mi
   - default (limits): cpu=300m, memory=256Mi
   - min: cpu=50m, memory=64Mi
   - max: cpu=500m, memory=512Mi
2. Run kubectl describe limitrange dev-limits -n team-a to confirm the settings.
3. Create a Pod named no-resources-pod in team-a without a resources block. (Image: nginx)
4. Check the Limits / Requests section of kubectl describe pod no-resources-pod -n team-a and confirm the LimitRange defaults were injected automatically.
5. Write a Pod manifest (over-limit-pod.yaml) with CPU limit set to 600m and try to create it with kubectl apply to observe the rejection error.
tasks.starter:
# 1. limit-range.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: dev-limits
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      defaultRequest:
        cpu: ____
        memory: ____
      default:
        cpu: ____
        memory: ____
      min:
        cpu: ____
        memory: ____
      max:
        cpu: ____
        memory: ____

# Apply
kubectl apply -f limit-range.yaml

# 3. Create a Pod without a resources block (manifest method)
# no-resources-pod.yaml (no resources block)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-resources-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

# 5. over-limit-pod.yaml (exceeds max)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: over-limit-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: ____
        limits:
          cpu: ____   # set to 600m to exceed max (500m)
tasks.solution:
# limit-range.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: dev-limits
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      defaultRequest:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
      default:
        cpu: 300m
        memory: 256Mi
      min:
        cpu: 50m
        memory: 64Mi
      max:
        cpu: 500m
        memory: 512Mi

# Apply
kubectl apply -f limit-range.yaml

# Verify settings
kubectl describe limitrange dev-limits -n team-a

# no-resources-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-resources-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

# Create and confirm defaults were injected
kubectl apply -f no-resources-pod.yaml
kubectl describe pod no-resources-pod -n team-a | grep -A6 'Limits\|Requests'
# Expected output:
#   Limits:
#     cpu:     300m
#     memory:  256Mi
#   Requests:
#     cpu:     100m
#     memory:  128Mi

# over-limit-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: over-limit-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 600m
        limits:
          cpu: 600m

# Attempt creation — confirm rejection
kubectl apply -f over-limit-pod.yaml
# Expected error:
# Error from server (Forbidden): error when creating "over-limit-pod.yaml": pods "over-limit-pod" is forbidden:
# [maximum cpu usage per Container is 500m, but limit is 600m]
tasks.explanation: LimitRange acts as an admission rule applied to the namespace. When a Pod is created without any resources block, the kube-apiserver injects the LimitRange's defaultRequest and default values automatically at the admission stage. This is why requests/limits you never specified appear in kubectl describe pod — that behavior is expected. A container that exceeds max is immediately rejected at the same admission stage, and the Pod object itself is never created. These two behaviors — automatic injection and upper-bound enforcement — are the core function of LimitRange.
hints: In a LimitRange, default sets the limits default and defaultRequest sets the requests default. They are separate fields and both must be specified. Using only default leaves requests unfilled, which causes problems when combined with ResourceQuota.
Use kubectl describe pod no-resources-pod -n team-a | grep -A6 Limits to quickly see just the Limits/Requests section. If there is no output, double-check the Pod name and namespace.

If the over-limit-pod error message contains 'maximum cpu usage per Container is 500m', the exercise is successful. If the Pod is created without error, verify that the max.cpu value is actually reflected in the LimitRange by running kubectl describe limitrange dev-limits -n team-a.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Applying ResourceQuota and Reproducing the Exceeded Quota Error
tasks.goal: Apply a ResourceQuota to team-a, then use kubectl apply to try to create a Pod that exceeds the quota and reproduce the 'exceeded quota' error firsthand. Also learn how to monitor current usage with describe quota.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write resource-quota.yaml with the following spec and apply it to team-a.
   - requests.cpu: 500m
   - requests.memory: 512Mi
   - limits.cpu: 1 (1 core)
   - limits.memory: 1Gi
   - pods: 3
2. Run kubectl describe quota team-quota -n team-a to verify usage vs. limits.
3. Write a Pod manifest (big-cpu-pod.yaml) requesting 600m CPU and try to create it with kubectl apply to observe the exceeded quota error. (Specify requests.cpu=600m in the resources block.)
4. The no-resources-pod from Step 2 already has LimitRange defaults applied (requests.cpu=100m). Confirm with describe quota that the current requests.cpu Used shows 100m.
5. Create two more Pods within normal bounds (small-pod-1, small-pod-2) to reach the pods limit of 3, then confirm that a 4th Pod is rejected.
tasks.starter:
# 1. resource-quota.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: ____
    requests.memory: ____
    limits.cpu: ____
    limits.memory: ____
    pods: ____

# 3. big-cpu-pod.yaml — set requests.cpu=600m to exceed the quota (500m)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: big-cpu-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: ____      # set to exceed the quota limit (500m)
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: ____
          memory: 256Mi

# 5. small-pod-1.yaml (within LimitRange bounds)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: small-pod-1
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 200m
          memory: 256Mi
tasks.solution:
# resource-quota.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: 500m
    requests.memory: 512Mi
    limits.cpu: "1"
    limits.memory: 1Gi
    pods: "3"

# Apply and verify
kubectl apply -f resource-quota.yaml
kubectl describe quota team-quota -n team-a
# Expected output (no-resources-pod already exists):
# Resource          Used   Hard
# --------          ----   ----
# limits.cpu        300m   1
# limits.memory     256Mi  1Gi
# pods              1      3
# requests.cpu      100m   500m
# requests.memory   128Mi  512Mi

# big-cpu-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: big-cpu-pod
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 600m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 600m
          memory: 256Mi

# Attempt creation — confirm exceeded quota
kubectl apply -f big-cpu-pod.yaml
# Expected error:
# Error from server (Forbidden): error when creating "big-cpu-pod.yaml": pods "big-cpu-pod" is forbidden:
# exceeded quota: team-quota, requested: requests.cpu=600m, used: requests.cpu=100m, limited: requests.cpu=500m

# small-pod-1.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: small-pod-1
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 200m
          memory: 256Mi

# small-pod-2.yaml (same spec as small-pod-1, only name changes)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: small-pod-2
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 200m
          memory: 256Mi

# Create both Pods (total of 3 including no-resources-pod — limit reached)
kubectl apply -f small-pod-1.yaml
kubectl apply -f small-pod-2.yaml

# Attempt 4th Pod — confirm pods limit exceeded
kubectl run extra-pod --image=nginx -n team-a
# Expected error:
# Error from server (Forbidden): pods "extra-pod" is forbidden:
# exceeded quota: team-quota, requested: pods=1, used: pods=3, limited: pods=3

# Re-check usage
kubectl describe quota team-quota -n team-a
tasks.explanation: The hard field in a ResourceQuota sets a ceiling on the aggregate requests/limits across all Pods in a namespace, as well as on object counts. A key insight is that the LimitRange-injected defaults on no-resources-pod (requests.cpu=100m) are also counted toward the quota's Used total. The exceeded quota error message helpfully shows all three values — requested, used, and limited — so you can immediately see how much is in use. When a compute resource quota such as requests.cpu is set on a namespace, every container in that namespace must explicitly declare requests/limits; LimitRange's default values satisfy this requirement automatically. This is the reason the two objects are always used together.
hints: For big-cpu-pod.yaml, the requests.cpu value must be high enough that used + requested exceeds hard. Since used is currently 100m from no-resources-pod, any new request of 401m or more pushes the total over 500m. Setting it to 600m makes the error easy to reproduce.

ResourceQuota does not apply retroactively to Pods that existed before it was created. However, the existing Pods' requests/limits values are still counted in the Used column of the new quota. Verify with describe quota's Used column.
To reproduce the pods limit exceeded error, exactly 3 Pods must be in Running or Pending state in the namespace. Run kubectl get pods -n team-a to check the count before attempting.

tasks.title: Advanced: Complete LimitRange + ResourceQuota Setup on a New Namespace
tasks.goal: Follow the production pattern to apply LimitRange first and then ResourceQuota on a new namespace, then verify end-to-end that Pods without a resources block are created without error and that Pods exceeding the quota are rejected.
tasks.prompt:
Build a complete multi-tenant environment in the team-b namespace in the following order.

1. Apply LimitRange (tb-limits)
   - defaultRequest: cpu=200m, memory=256Mi
   - default (limits): cpu=400m, memory=512Mi
   - max: cpu=800m, memory=1Gi
   - min: cpu=100m, memory=128Mi

2. Apply ResourceQuota (tb-quota)
   - requests.cpu: 800m
   - requests.memory: 1Gi
   - limits.cpu: 2 (2 cores)
   - limits.memory: 2Gi
   - pods: 4

3. Create 3 Pods (app-1, app-2, app-3) in team-b without a resources block. (Image: nginx)

4. Run kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b to check current usage.
   - Confirm that requests.cpu Used is close to 200m * 3 = 600m (the result of LimitRange defaults being injected automatically).

5. Try to create a 4th Pod (app-4) without a resources block. Since the 3 existing Pods are already consuming 600m via the LimitRange default (requests.cpu=200m), adding app-4's 200m brings the total to exactly 800m — the limit.
   - The total exactly equals the limit, so app-4 should be allowed. After creating it, confirm Used is 800m.

6. Try to create a 5th Pod (app-5) and confirm the exceeded quota error (either the pods limit of 4 or the requests.cpu limit of 800m is hit first).

7. Capture the final output of kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b and note the Used/Hard values.
tasks.starter:
# 1. tb-limits.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: tb-limits
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      defaultRequest:
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 256Mi
      default:
        cpu: 400m
        memory: 512Mi
      max:
        cpu: 800m
        memory: 1Gi
      min:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi

# 2. tb-quota.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: tb-quota
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: 800m
    requests.memory: 1Gi
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 2Gi
    pods: "4"

# 3. app-1.yaml (no resources block — same structure for app-2, app-3)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-1
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx
      # resources block intentionally omitted
tasks.solution:
# Apply tb-limits.yaml
kubectl apply -f tb-limits.yaml

# Apply tb-quota.yaml
kubectl apply -f tb-quota.yaml

# app-1.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-1
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

# app-2.yaml (name changed to app-2, rest identical)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-2
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

# app-3.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-3
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

# Create all 3 Pods
kubectl apply -f app-1.yaml
kubectl apply -f app-2.yaml
kubectl apply -f app-3.yaml

# 4. Check usage
kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b
# Expected output:
# Resource          Used   Hard
# --------          ----   ----
# limits.cpu        1200m  2
# limits.memory     1536Mi 2Gi
# pods              3      4
# requests.cpu      600m   800m
# requests.memory   768Mi  1Gi

# 5. Create app-4 (requests.cpu 600m + 200m = 800m, exactly at limit)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-4
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

kubectl apply -f app-4.yaml
# Creation succeeds (800m == 800m limit, equal is allowed)

kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b
# requests.cpu Used: 800m  Hard: 800m  (limit 100% used)
# pods         Used: 4     Hard: 4     (limit 100% used)

# 6. Attempt app-5 — confirm exceeded quota
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-5
  namespace: team-b
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx

kubectl apply -f app-5.yaml
# Expected error (whichever limit is hit first — pods or requests.cpu):
# Error from server (Forbidden): pods "app-5" is forbidden:
# exceeded quota: tb-quota, requested: pods=1, used: pods=4, limited: pods=4

# 7. Final usage check
kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b
tasks.explanation: This advanced exercise is designed to walk through the full production workflow end-to-end: namespace creation → LimitRange → ResourceQuota → workload deployment → usage monitoring. There are two key observations. First, 3 Pods without a resources block are created without any error — this is because LimitRange transparently fills in the defaultRequest (cpu=200m) to satisfy ResourceQuota's requirement that compute resources be explicitly declared. Second, when the limit is reached, exactly the one Pod that would exceed it is rejected — this is the mechanism that guarantees fair resource distribution across teams. In production, this configuration is typically packaged in a Helm chart or Kustomize overlay and applied consistently to every new team or environment.
hints: tb-limits must be applied before tb-quota. Reversing the order causes Pods without resources blocks to be immediately rejected by tb-quota's explicit declaration requirement. The LimitRange → ResourceQuota order matters.

app-4 exactly meets the requests.cpu limit (800m), so it is allowed. Exceeding the limit triggers rejection; equaling the limit does not. app-5 is rejected because the pods limit (4) is already full before requests.cpu is even evaluated.

The Used/Hard columns side-by-side in kubectl describe quota tb-quota -n team-b make it immediately clear which resource is the bottleneck. This command is the first thing to check when troubleshooting CI/CD pipeline scheduling failures.
